alert_title: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO, intermittent 500s

timeline.event: Deploy: payment-service v5.3.0 (changelog: tuned outbound HTTP client connection pool sizing for charge-service calls; structured logging for charge events)
Deploy: auth-service v2.9.0 (changelog: integrate fraud-score-service risk check into token validation flow)
Alert fired: payment-service /charge p99 latency above SLO

logs_excerpt: redis: INFO charge-cache hit rate at 90% (typically 97%)
payment-service: WARN /charge request queued 1200ms waiting for worker thread
postgres: INFO query latency nominal, no entries in slow query log
payment-service: WARN call to auth-service for token validation took 1840ms (typically under 100ms)
payment-service: INFO outbound connection pool to fraud-related endpoints: 0 idle, 40/40 in use
payment-service: INFO outbound connection pool to all other endpoints: 35/40 idle

candidate_causes.label: auth-service v2.9.0 integrated a synchronous fraud-score-service risk check into the token validation flow, adding ~1700ms per call; payment-service's outbound pool to fraud-related endpoints is fully saturated while its pool to all other endpoints stays mostly idle, isolating the bottleneck to exactly the auth-service-dependent calls
candidate_causes.mechanism: The fraud-score-service lookup added to auth-service's validate_token path adds roughly 1700ms per call (auth-service's own p99 jumped from a 95ms baseline to 1840ms). Critically, payment-service's outbound connection pool is segmented by endpoint class: the pool serving fraud-related (auth) endpoints is at 100% utilization while the pool serving every other endpoint sits at only 12%. A general capacity or pool-sizing problem would show exhaustion across both pools; this asymmetry means only the auth-service-dependent path is starved, pointing directly at auth-service's new lookup as the cause rather than anything in payment-service itself.

candidate_causes.label: payment-service's own v5.3.0 deploy retuned outbound connection pool sizing, and that retuning is what caused the worker pool exhaustion
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's a deploy to the symptomatic service itself, shipped shortly before the alert, and its changelog explicitly mentions changing connection pool sizing -- the exact component implicated in the symptom, not just a generic recent-deploy coincidence.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the pool-usage asymmetry: if the v5.3.0 retuning had misconfigured pooling broadly, the 'other endpoints' pool would show the same exhaustion as the fraud-endpoints pool. It doesn't (12% vs 100% usage), so the retuning itself isn't the bottleneck; only the specific downstream dependency (auth-service) is.

candidate_causes.label: Degraded Redis cache performance is slowing down charge processing
candidate_causes.why_tempting: redis_cache_hit_rate_pct genuinely dropped from a 97% baseline to 90% concurrent with the incident -- a real, measurable anomaly, not an obviously-nominal stat to dismiss.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because the hit-rate dip is a downstream effect, not a trigger: timed-out and retried charge requests recompute pricing instead of reading from a warm cache, producing exactly this kind of secondary hit-rate drop. postgres_query_latency_p50_ms staying nominal at 13ms confirms the database Redis would otherwise be protecting was never under real pressure, meaning the cache dip doesn't reflect genuine load needing a cache assist -- it's a symptom of the same incident, not its cause.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Make the fraud-score-service lookup in auth-service's validate_token path asynchronous or cached, or wrap it in a strict timeout + circuit breaker -- do not revert payment-service's v5.3.0 connection pool retuning (it isn't the cause) and do not scale Redis (its hit-rate dip is a symptom, not the trigger).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 2: both distractors now share literal evidence overlap with the true cause rather than being trivially nominal -- the deploy distractor explicitly touches 'connection pool sizing' (the same component implicated in the symptom), and the Redis distractor has a real, measured anomaly (97%->90%) rather than an obviously-healthy stat. Discrimination requires noticing the pool-usage asymmetry between endpoint classes (ruling out a pool-wide misconfiguration) and recognizing the cache-hit dip as a downstream effect of retried requests rather than a cause -- both require reasoning about evidence direction, not just presence/absence of an anomaly.

alert_title: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%, intermittent timeouts on add-to-cart

timeline.event: Deploy: cart-service v4.2.0 (changelog: localized UI copy strings; minor adjustment to client-side retry/backoff timing for localization-fallback API calls)
[external status feed] OktaCorp (identity provider): investigating elevated latency on token-introspection API
auth-service p99 latency on external identity-provider calls climbs to 2200ms (baseline 80ms)
Alert fired: cart-service checkout error rate above 5%

logs_excerpt: cart-service: ERROR add-to-cart request failed after retry exhaustion (14 occurrences in 5m)
cart-service: INFO redis-cache GET for cart-session averaging 6ms (typically 4ms)
cart-service: WARN payment pre-check call to payment-service timed out after 3000ms (32 occurrences in 5m)
payment-service: WARN worker pool queue depth climbing, 91% utilization
auth-service: INFO CPU and memory within normal range
payment-service: WARN call to auth-service exceeded 2000ms on 58 of last 100 requests
auth-service: WARN identity-provider introspection call latency elevated, p99 2200ms (typically 80ms)

candidate_causes.label: OktaCorp's token-introspection endpoint degraded (per its own status feed, timestamp-correlated with auth-service's latency climb), increasing auth-service's validation latency; that queued payment-service's calls to auth-service and exhausted its worker pool, cascading into cart-service checkout timeouts two hops upstream
candidate_causes.mechanism: auth-service's own CPU and memory are normal, ruling out a local resource problem; its latency spike to external identity-provider calls specifically (2200ms vs an 80ms baseline) lines up in time with OktaCorp's own status-feed report of introspection-endpoint degradation, meaning the slowdown is external. Because payment-service holds a worker thread for the duration of each auth-service call, that latency consumed payment-service's pool (91% utilization), and because cart-service's checkout flow makes a synchronous payment pre-check call to payment-service, the exhaustion propagated two hops upstream into cart-service's own timeouts -- without any code or infrastructure change inside cart-service itself.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's v4.2.0 deploy, which adjusted client-side retry/backoff timing, introduced the checkout regression
candidate_causes.why_tempting: It's the most recent deploy to the symptomatic service itself, and it explicitly touches retry/backoff timing -- the same kind of timeout/retry behavior visible in the symptom, not just an unrelated cosmetic change.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out by the changelog specifying the retry/backoff change applies only to localization-fallback API calls, a separate code path from the payment pre-check that's actually timing out, and by cart_service_cpu_pct remaining a normal 38%.

candidate_causes.label: cart-service's Redis cache is degraded, slowing down checkout
candidate_causes.why_tempting: cart_service_redis_get_latency_ms genuinely rose from a 4ms baseline to 6ms concurrent with the incident -- a real, measured deviation, not an obviously-nominal stat, and Redis is cart-service's own direct, visible dependency.
candidate_causes.mechanism: N/A -- ruled out because a 4ms->6ms shift is far too small to explain 3000ms timeouts, and cart_service_redis_hit_rate_pct stays healthy at 94%; the mild latency uptick is consistent with cart-service's own threads being backed up waiting on the payment pre-check (a downstream effect of the real bottleneck), not a degraded cache.

ground_truth.correct_remediation: Add a timeout + circuit breaker (with a fail-open or cached fallback) around auth-service's calls to the identity provider's introspection endpoint so a third-party degradation can't block payment-service and cart-service; do not revert cart-service v4.2.0 (its retry change is on an unrelated code path) and do not scale cart-service's Redis (a 4ms->6ms shift isn't the bottleneck).
ground_truth.difficulty_notes: Tier 3: true cause is two hops upstream (cart-service -> payment-service -> auth-service -> external identity provider) and the external trigger is no longer stated as a cause inside any service's own logs -- it's a separate status-feed timeline event that must be timestamp-correlated with auth-service's latency climb, not read off a single line. Both distractors now carry genuine, measured (if small) anomalies sharing literal vocabulary with the true cause (retry/backoff timing; latency uptick) rather than being trivially nominal, forcing the responder to judge magnitude and direction (cause vs. downstream effect) rather than just presence of an anomaly.
